preamble: The shift from a retroflex stop [ʈ] to an alveolar stop [t] is a form of de-retroflexion. It involves moving the point of articulation forward (apical displacement) from the hard palate to the alveolar ridge. This is a common form of phonological simplification in languages with marginal retroflex series or through areal contact with non-retroflex languages.

examples.note: Historical merger where retroflex and alveolar stops merged into a simpler dental/alveolar series in specific branches.
Historical simplification where retroflex clusters fronted to dental or alveolar geminates.
Ongoing de-retroflexion in younger speakers who neutralize the contrast between alveolar and retroflex stops.
